repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
When isinstance() is called with a walrus operator as the first argument and a tuple of types, e.g. ``isinstance(ni := next(it), (int, float, str, bytes))``, Cython's isinstance-tuple optimization expands this into a short-circuit OR of individual type checks. Each branch of that OR tree independently calls ``generate_evaluation_code`` on the first argument, causing the walrus expression to be re-evaluated once per type — calling ``next(it)`` four times per loop iteration instead of once.

Root cause: ``_handle_simple_function_isinstance`` wraps the arg in a ``ResultRefNode`` (evaluated-once cache) only when ``not arg.is_simple()``. A walrus operator reaches the optimizer wrapped in a ``CoerceToTempNode``, which reports ``is_simple() = True`` (because ``is_temp = True``), so the caching is incorrectly skipped.

Fix: unwrap any ``CoercionNode`` layers before the ``is_simple()`` check and force ``ResultRefNode`` caching when the underlying expression is an ``AssignmentExpressionNode``.

comment_data.body:
I wonder if it would be easier to make a shorter list of what's allowed. Maybe

```
if not (arg.is_literal or (arg.is_name and not arg.is_temp)):
    # wrap in result-ref-node
```

It's doing the wrong this in quite a few cases. Even for a global name it's looking it up twice, which works but is not really optimal.

`is_simple` isn't really the right check here because it does catch most nodes that evaluate to temps (except call nodes which are special-cased)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -2977,7 +2977,14 @@ def _handle_simple_function_isinstance(self, node, function, pos_args):
         # Map the separate type checks to check functions.
 
         if types and (allowed_none_node or len(types) > 1):
-            if arg.is_attribute or not arg.is_simple():
+            # CoerceToTempNode wrapping a walrus operator (AssignmentExpressionNode) reports
+            # is_simple()=True (is_temp=True), but each call to generate_evaluation_code()
+            # re-evaluates the underlying expression. Unwrap any CoercionNode layers to detect
+            # this case and force the arg into a ResultRefNode so it is evaluated only once.
+            inner = arg
+            while isinstance(inner, ExprNodes.CoercionNode):
+                inner = inner.arg
+            if arg.is_attribute or not arg.is_simple() or isinstance(inner, ExprNodes.AssignmentExpressionNode):

comment_data.body: It seems generally fuzzy what that positive list should look like, so I created https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7671 for coming up with a general scheme to mark such nodes.

comment_data.body: Unnecessary temp assignment is harmful for Python objects because it requires reference counting.

comment_data.body:
> Unnecessary temp assignment is harmful for Python objects because it requires reference counting.

I see, so I guess there are cases where the temp can outlive the original reference?

comment_data.body:
> I see, so I guess there are cases where the temp can outlive the original reference?

There are certain cases in general - and this is one of the reasons we use temps. I'm not sure if there's any case here.

But our temporary handling is built around the idea that the temporary owns a reference. Usually this is correct anyway (e.g. the result of function call is an owned reference).  But for something simple like a function local variable it would add an incref and decref.

commit.message:
Fix walrus operator re-evaluated multiple times in isinstance() tuple optimization

When isinstance() is called with a walrus operator as the first argument and a
tuple of types, e.g. ``isinstance(ni := next(it), (int, float, str, bytes))``,
Cython's isinstance-tuple optimization expands this into a short-circuit OR of
individual type checks. Each branch of that OR tree independently calls
``generate_evaluation_code`` on the first argument, causing the walrus
expression to be re-evaluated once per type — calling ``next(it)`` four times
per loop iteration instead of once.

Root cause: ``_handle_simple_function_isinstance`` wraps the arg in a
``ResultRefNode`` (evaluated-once cache) only when ``not arg.is_simple()``.
A walrus operator reaches the optimizer wrapped in a ``CoerceToTempNode``,
which reports ``is_simple() = True`` (because ``is_temp = True``), so the
caching is incorrectly skipped.

Fix: unwrap any ``CoercionNode`` layers before the ``is_simple()`` check and
force ``ResultRefNode`` caching when the underlying expression is an
``AssignmentExpressionNode``.

Fix walrus operator re-evaluated multiple times in isinstance() tuple optimization

When isinstance() is called with a walrus operator as the first argument and a
tuple of types, e.g. ``isinstance(ni := next(it), (int, float, str, bytes))``,
Cython's isinstance-tuple optimization expands this into a short-circuit OR of
individual type checks. Each branch of that OR tree independently calls
``generate_evaluation_code`` on the first argument, causing the walrus
expression to be re-evaluated once per type — calling ``next(it)`` four times
per loop iteration instead of once.

Root cause: ``_handle_simple_function_isinstance`` wraps the arg in a
``ResultRefNode`` (evaluated-once cache) only when ``not arg.is_simple()``.
A walrus operator reaches the optimizer wrapped in a ``CoerceToTempNode``,
which reports ``is_simple() = True`` (because ``is_temp = True``), so the
caching is incorrectly skipped.

Fix: unwrap any ``CoercionNode`` layers before the ``is_simple()`` check and
force ``ResultRefNode`` caching when the underlying expression is an
``AssignmentExpressionNode``.

commit.message:
Generalise isinstance() arg-caching condition to an allowlist

The previous fix for the walrus-operator case detected
``CoercionNode(AssignmentExpressionNode)`` specifically.  A reviewer noted that
the problem is broader: attributes, global names, calls, and any other
expression that isn't a literal or a plain local name would also be evaluated
once per type in the short-circuit OR chain generated for
``isinstance(expr, (A, B, C))``.

Replace the blocklist-style check
    ``arg.is_attribute or not arg.is_simple() or <walrus special-case>``
with a tight allowlist:
    ``not (arg.is_literal or (arg.is_name and not arg.is_temp))``

Only literals and non-temporary name lookups (i.e. simple local variables) are
genuinely safe to reference more than once without caching.  Everything else is
wrapped in a ResultRefNode so it is evaluated exactly once before the OR
branches run.

Only literals and non-temporary name lookups (i.e. simple local variables) are
